lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operating companies primarily engaged in commercially catching finfish from natural habitat and repeatedly selling landed catch to external processors, dealers, wholesalers, auctions, restaurants, or other buyers, including vessel operation, navigation, gear deployment, catch handling, and required reporting.
lens.excluded_activities: Aquaculture and hatcheries; shellfish and other marine-product fishing; recreational charter services; seafood processing detached from the fishing operation; passive vessel or permit ownership; captive internal fleets; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Trip- and season-based product revenue from ex-vessel sales of wild finfish, often through repeat dealer or processor relationships. Realized revenue depends on landed weight, species, grade, market price, quota or allocation, season, bycatch rules, fuel and crew-share economics, and permit access.

a.bridge: BLS describes fishing occupations and NOAA documents electronic reporting, monitoring, and machine-vision capabilities, but neither measures the wage-weighted not-yet-automated task share for finfish firms. I map voyage planning, fish-location support, catch imaging, measurement, logbooks, compliance, sales administration, and maintenance prediction into exposed work while retaining vessel command, gear handling, repairs, catch handling, safety, and physical deck work; technology examples may overstate exposure on small vessels and in visually difficult fisheries.
a.rationale: Captains already use sonar, radar, GPS, and fish-finding equipment, creating a digital base for better prediction and decision support. NOAA shows that reporting and monitoring can be digitized and that AI can automate video review and species measurement. Most deck labor remains physical, variable, safety-critical, and performed in harsh conditions, limiting substitution.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study for finfish vessels by gear, vessel size, and fishery, separating current automation from remaining navigation, search, deck, sorting, maintenance, compliance, sales, and management work.
caveats: BLS combines fishing and hunting workers and does not publish a finfish-specific wage-weighted occupation mix.
NOAA electronic monitoring primarily serves data collection and compliance, so labor savings may accrue partly to regulators or observers rather than vessel operators.
The frozen l uses wages and receipts from different vintages and an IPS harmonization factor, which can misstate current labor intensity.

rho.bridge: NOAA describes deployed electronic systems and ongoing machine-vision development across fisheries, not the five-year implementation share of exposed work in eligible finfish companies. I give established navigation and reporting tools higher implementation weight and research-stage automated image interpretation lower weight, then discount for saltwater reliability, bandwidth, retrofit cost, crew workflow, model accuracy, cyber risk, and fishery-specific regulatory approval; national program momentum may overstate adoption by small independent vessels.
rho.rationale: Electronic reporting, cameras, GPS, vessel monitoring, and gear sensors are operational foundations, and automated image processing can reduce manual review. Implementation is plausible for administrative and decision-support tasks, but machine vision, integration, and at-sea reliability remain uneven and no evidence supports broad autonomous harvesting.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year fleet cohort data by fishery on installed electronic and AI systems, capital and service costs, uptime, data-review hours, crew hours avoided, false classifications, regulatory acceptance, fuel savings, and system abandonment.
caveats: Regulatory mandates can accelerate adoption without creating an operator-level economic return.
Systems proven on conveyor-equipped vessels may not translate to small boats or catch handled irregularly on deck.
Connectivity, corrosion, vibration, lighting, species mix, and weather can degrade field performance.

e.rationale: Commercial finfish operations have repeat external buyers and potentially transferable vessels, permits, quota, crews, and customer relationships. Eligibility is reduced when the captain-owner is indispensable, permits are personal or nontransferable, revenue spans multiple fishing codes, books rely on crew shares, or profitability depends on volatile allocations. Public data do not identify normalized EBITDA or enterprise boundaries for the frozen firm set.
e.change_evidence: Business-level diligence on the ten frozen candidate firms covering normalized EBITDA, species revenue mix, legal entities, permit and quota ownership, vessel condition, captain dependence, crew arrangements, buyer concentration, and transfer restrictions.
caveats: The frozen n equals 10 but is an estimate derived using a broad Farming and Agriculture EBITDA margin rather than observed finfish-company profitability.
NAICS establishment classification may split or combine vessels differently from a transferable enterprise.
A vessel and permit can be saleable even when the operating company lacks transferable management or stable earnings.

s5.bridge: NOAA documents a national graying-of-the-fleet problem, Northeast crew tenure, and one transferable West Coast groundfish permit regime, but does not measure qualifying five-year control transfers of eligible finfish firms. I translate aging and entry barriers into elevated succession pressure, then discount for family succession, vessel-only sales, permit leasing, nontransferable rights, liquidation, and owner-captain businesses that cease rather than transfer; the regional sources may overstate or understate other fisheries.
s5.rationale: NOAA reports many commercial fishermen approaching retirement and high startup barriers for new entrants. Its Northeast survey shows few young entrants and long crew tenure, while the West Coast groundfish example demonstrates that some limited-entry permits and ownership can transfer. These conditions support turnover, but not every transition is a qualifying sale of operating control.
s5.change_evidence: National or regional permit-registry microdata linked to vessel and beneficial ownership changes, brokered vessel sales, quota transfers, owner age, crew continuity, and operating-company survival, coded by finfish fishery over five years.
caveats: The crew survey covers New England and the Mid-Atlantic and includes hired captains rather than owners alone.
Permit transferability and ownership rules vary substantially by fishery and jurisdiction.
Retirement may produce family succession, lease, vessel sale, permit sale, or liquidation rather than a company control transaction.

q.rationale: Finfish businesses usually sell price-sensitive landed product rather than bill a service fee, so dealers, processors, auctions, competing vessels, and imported seafood can capture part of any cost advantage. Catch limits and scarce transferable access can restrict supply and preserve operator economics, while better trip selection, fuel efficiency, quality, and compliance may remain firm-specific. Retention therefore varies sharply by fishery and buyer structure.
q.change_evidence: Matched trip-level data on ex-vessel prices, catch, fuel, crew shares, buyer contracts, quality premiums, quota costs, and technology adoption, plus buyer concentration and renewal behavior by port and species.
caveats: No source directly observes discounted five-year retention of an implemented AI benefit for finfish operators.
Imported seafood affects downstream prices but is not a like-for-like substitute for every domestic species and quality grade.
Crew-share arrangements can distribute savings or added catch differently from conventional payroll businesses.

d5.bridge: NOAA seafood consumption, imports, and stock status describe broad seafood demand and biological supply, while BLS projects fishing-and-hunting employment, not constant-price demand for the exact finfish-catching basket. I treat seafood consumption as a stable end-market anchor but cap domestic wild-catch growth for quotas, stock conditions, climate shifts, imports, and aquaculture; employment decline may overstate demand erosion when productivity or consolidation reduces labor.
d5.rationale: U.S. consumers ate 19.1 pounds of seafood per person in 2023, but imports supplied an estimated 80 percent. NOAA found most assessed stocks were not subject to overfishing or overfished, yet meaningful numbers remain constrained and catch limits bind quantity. BLS expects occupation employment to decline, supporting a cautious near-flat base with substantial fishery-specific downside and upside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year species-level forecasts for allowable biological catch, quota, climate distribution, landings, ex-vessel demand, imports, aquaculture substitution, and processor capacity, weighted to each eligible firm's actual revenue mix.
caveats: Seafood consumption combines finfish, shellfish, imports, and aquaculture and is not a direct measure of domestic wild-finfish demand.
National stock counts do not weight stocks by the revenue mix of eligible firms.
Climate-driven range shifts, closures, rebuilding plans, and quota changes can create much larger local swings than the national range.

o.rationale: Wild finfish still must be caught and landed by an accountable vessel operation that manages navigation, safety, permits, gear, crew, catch handling, and compliance. AI can improve decisions and documentation but does not eliminate the operator role within five years. A small allowance covers quantity shifting to aquaculture, vertically integrated fleets, or structures outside the lens.
o.change_evidence: Commercial deployment of autonomous fishing vessels with accepted regulatory accountability, or evidence that processors and aquaculture replace independent finfish operators while preserving the same consumed quantity.
caveats: This is a structural estimate rather than an observed operator-displacement rate.
Remote monitoring and decision support change operator work but do not transfer navigational and safety accountability to software.
Quantity loss or substitution is reflected primarily in d5 rather than duplicated here.

narrative.executive_view: Finfish fishing has a credible but bounded digital opportunity in route and location support, electronic reporting, catch monitoring, machine vision, compliance, and administration. Physical deck work, safety-critical vessel operations, quota and stock constraints, commodity pricing, permit complexity, and owner-captain dependence limit both labor substitution and transferability.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can combine weather, ocean, sonar, historical catch, and market data to support trip planning, while vision systems can help identify, count, and measure catch and reduce manual video review. It can also automate logbooks, document checks, maintenance alerts, and buyer administration. Gear handling, sorting in irregular conditions, repairs, navigation accountability, and emergency response remain human work.
narrative.value_capture: Value can come from fewer unproductive vessel hours, lower fuel use, better targeting within legal limits, reduced reporting burden, improved quality, and fewer compliance errors. Buyers and competing vessels may capture part of cost savings through ex-vessel pricing, while scarce permits and catch rights can preserve more value in constrained fisheries.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates only ten firms in the target earnings band, and the margin bridge is not fishery-specific. Aging participants and high entry costs create transition pressure, but permits, quota, vessels, and operating entities may transfer separately, and owner-captain knowledge can be difficult to replace.
narrative.demand_durability: Seafood demand is established, but imported product and aquaculture meet much of U.S. consumption. Domestic wild-finfish quantity depends as much on biological stocks, catch limits, climate-driven range shifts, protected-species rules, and processor capacity as on consumer appetite.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The most material uncertainties are fishery and gear heterogeneity, current automation by vessel class, field reliability, regulatory acceptance, cyber and connectivity risk, fuel and quota economics, volatile landings and prices, climate and stock changes, buyer concentration, crew-share accounting, permit transfer rules, and the dataset's broad-margin estimate of n.
narrative.principal_driver: Digital navigation, reporting, monitoring, and catch-analysis workflows can reduce wasted trips and administrative or review labor on costly vessels.
narrative.principal_weakness: Biological and regulatory quantity constraints plus owner-captain and permit dependence make outcomes highly fishery-specific and can prevent a clean transfer of operating control.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual
sources.supports: Industry boundary for commercial catching of finfish from natural habitat and exclusions for aquaculture and seafood processing.

sources.title: Fishing and Hunting Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Fishing duties, captain and deckhand work, navigation technology, physical tasks, licensing, retirement replacement, and employment outlook.

sources.title: Electronic Technologies
sources.supports: Electronic reporting, electronic monitoring, vessel tracking, gear sensors, and AI-assisted video processing in fisheries.

sources.title: Developing Machine Vision to Collect More Timely Fisheries Data
sources.supports: Machine-vision development for identifying and measuring commercial catch and the operational challenges of electronic monitoring.

sources.title: Fisheries of the United States
sources.supports: Commercial landings and value, seafood consumption, import reliance, aquaculture output, regions, and leading species.

sources.title: Status of Stocks 2024
sources.supports: Managed-stock counts, overfishing and overfished status, catch-limit compliance, rebuilding, and biological demand constraints.

sources.title: Building the Next Generation of U.S. Commercial Fishermen
sources.supports: Retirement pressure, graying of the fleet, high entry costs, and succession challenges.

sources.title: 2026 Commercial Fishing Crew Survey
sources.supports: Regional evidence on young entrants, crew tenure, family participation, and consideration of leaving fishing.

sources.title: Groundfish Limited Entry Change of Ownership and Vessel Registration
sources.supports: One finfish permit regime's limited-entry, ownership-change, vessel-registration, leasing, and transferability rules.
